title: Der Struwwelpeter
source: Heinrich Hoffmann, Der Struwwelpeter, Rütten & Loening, Frankfurt am Main, 564. Auflage — the canonical later text in modernized spelling (via Project Gutenberg #24571). Fresh Lectorium translation from the German.
about: The title figure himself: Peter, who for nearly a year has let no one cut his nails or comb his hair. In the book he stands on a pedestal as frontispiece and warning — the mildest of its sinners, punished only by everyone’s »pfui!«. His name — roughly ‘Shaggy Peter’ — became the book’s title and, in English, ‘Shock-headed Peter.’

sentences.t:
Sieh einmal, hier steht er,
pfui, der Struwwelpeter!
sentences.l:
See once, here stands he,
ugh, the Struwwelpeter!
sentences.i:
Just look — here he stands:
ugh, Struwwelpeter!
sentences.n: »einmal« after an imperative = ‘just (look)’ — the same particle as Grimm’s »Seht einmal«. »pfui« is the German cry of disgust: ‘ugh!, for shame!’. And the article before the name again (»der Struwwelpeter«), as with »der Konrad« — the name itself means roughly ‘Shaggy-Peter,’ from »struwwelig« ‘tousled, unkempt.’

»sich schneiden lassen« — lassen + infinitive again, here in refusal: ‘he did not let (them) be cut.’ The word order »An den Händen beiden« (‘on the hands both’) is inverted to rhyme with »schneiden«. Time expressions like »fast ein Jahr« stand in the bare accusative — no preposition ‘for.’

sentences.t: kämmen ließ er nicht sein Haar.
sentences.l: To-comb let he not his hair.
sentences.i: and his hair he won’t let be combed.
sentences.n: The same lassen-refusal, now with the infinitive »kämmen« flung to the front of the sentence for emphasis: combing is the thing he will not have.

sentences.t:
Pfui, ruft da ein jeder:
Garstger Struwwelpeter!
sentences.l:
Ugh, calls there every-one:
nasty Struwwelpeter!
sentences.n: »ein jeder« = ‘each and every one’ — the emphatic form of »jeder«. »Garstger« = »garstiger« (‘nasty, horrid’) with the -i- squeezed out for the meter; this edition drops the apostrophe older printings use (Garst’ger). His whole punishment is public disgust — no scissors-man comes for Peter.
